provenance.source_corpus: Legacy Arsenic Pollution of Lakes Near Cobalt, Ontario, Canada: Arsenic in Lake Water and Sediment Remains Elevated Nearly a Century After Mining Activity Has Ceased

source_spans.text: Although it is not disputed that historical mining activity has increased arsenic concentrations in certain lakes around Cobalt
Although it is not disputed that historical mining activity has increased arsenic concentrations in certain lakes around Cobalt

Fe-Mn oxides at the water-sediment interface perpetually scavenge arsenic from buried tailings below and from contaminated surface waters that cause arsenic concentrations to remain enriched in the upper sediments even after tailings have been buried by lake sediment.
Even though background concentrations are naturally elevated, the presence of arsenic-rich tailings in these lakes has prevented any natural recovery from occurring.
Even though background concentrations are naturally elevated, the presence of arsenic-rich tailings in these lakes has prevented any natural recovery from occurring.

nodes.name: Fe-Mn oxides at the water-sediment interface

source_spans.text: Fe-Mn oxides at the water-sediment interface perpetually scavenge arsenic from buried tailings below and from contaminated surface waters that cause arsenic concentrations to remain enriched in the upper sediments even after tailings have been buried by lake sediment.
Migration of tailings from these locations is substantial and controlled by the streams and creeks that run through the low-lying tailings deposits, transporting them downstream.
This lack of containment has led to the contamination of several lakes in the area that had no historical mining activity in their catchment
Even if physical barriers exist (e.g., beaver dams) effluent from tailings deposits can significantly alter surface water and lake sediment geochemistry in lakes downstream.

nodes.name: surface water and lake sediment geochemistry
